title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Income poverty




The first analytical layer of the monitor turns the annual ENEMDU poverty output into a compact reading of income, territory, and reproducibility.

In 2025, Ecuador’s annual income poverty rate reached 21.95%. The national figure is the starting point, but the substantive story is territorial: poverty reached 16.51% in urban areas and 33.63% in rural areas.




This page uses the curated site output data/derived/site/site_kpis.rds. It presents aggregated indicators only: no raw microdata, no private paths, and no individual identifiers.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text: This page uses the curated site output data/derived/site/site_kpis.rds. It presents aggregated indicators only: no raw microdata, no private paths, and no individual identifiers.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Income poverty
The annual estimate shows that roughly one in five people lived in households whose per-capita income did not reach the income poverty line. The national rate was 21.95%.
The monitor treats this figure as an annual structural reading, not as a monthly pulse. That distinction matters because the annual ENEMDU file supports a broader territorial and thematic analysis than short-term releases.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Urban-rural gap
The urban-rural contrast is the strongest signal in this first layer. Urban income poverty reached 16.51%, while rural income poverty reached 33.63%.
That gap is not only a difference in levels. It points to different policy questions: labor income and cost of living in urban areas, and a more structural combination of income generation, access, and territorial vulnerability in rural areas.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Annual KPI summary


Income poverty
National: 21.95%
Urban: 16.51%
Rural: 33.63%


Extreme income poverty
National: 6.99%
Urban: 3.69%
Rural: 14.07%


Benchmark status
All six annual KPI rows are marked as annual_published_table_close_match in the curated site output.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
A deeper income threshold
Extreme income poverty reached 6.99% nationally in 2025. The territorial contrast is again sharp: 3.69% in urban areas and 14.07% in rural areas.
This layer separates income poverty from extreme income poverty because the two indicators answer different questions. Poverty identifies households below the income poverty line; extreme poverty identifies households below a more restrictive minimum threshold.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Benchmark comparison
The annual output was compared against public 2025 annual INEC tabulations. The published benchmark values are close to the monitor estimates after rounding: 21.8% for national income poverty, 16.3% for urban income poverty, 33.4% for rural income poverty, 6.8% for national extreme poverty, 3.6% for urban extreme poverty, and 13.7% for rural extreme poverty.
The comparison is a reproducibility check against a public reference table. It is not an institutional endorsement or a replacement for official statistical releases.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
section: What this page does not claim
text:
What this page does not claim
This page does not estimate causality, explain every driver of poverty, or create a new official statistic. It presents a stable annual analytical layer built from a derived, aggregated site output.
The next versions of the monitor should add uncertainty, representativity notes, and deeper territorial detail only where the survey design supports those domains.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Next analytical layers
The next income-poverty layer can add poverty-line context, precision indicators, historical comparisons, and distributional readings. Deprivation, multidimensional poverty, labor, and province-level analysis should remain separate layers until their own output contracts are complete.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Poverty intelligence for Ecuador



Every time a poverty rate is published, the country can appear to fit inside one percentage point. Ecuador does not fit inside one number.
This monitor organizes income poverty, deprivation, territory, labor conditions, and statistical quality into one reproducible editorial product.

Start with the purpose Leer en español





The monitor separates short-term signals from structural poverty analysis, so comparisons stay meaningful.

Pulse

National, urban/rural, and labor-market context.




Quarterly

Cities

Quito, Guayaquil, Cuenca, Machala, and Ambato when the domain supports it.




Annual

Structure

Poverty, income distribution, NBI, multidimensional poverty, and provincial territory.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Monthly pulse
Latest available month
Short-term context for national and urban/rural indicators, especially labor-market conditions.
This layer helps read the present without confusing volatility with structure.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Quarterly view
Five main cities
Intermediate monitoring for Quito, Guayaquil, Cuenca, Machala, and Ambato when the sample design supports it.
This layer surfaces urban signals more frequently than the annual analysis.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Annual core
Full structure
Income poverty, quintiles, NBI, multidimensional poverty, provinces, cities, and territorial analysis.
This is the narrative foundation of the monitor.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
2025 annual income poverty snapshot
In 2025, Ecuador’s annual income poverty rate reached 21.95%. The annual reading shows a strong territorial contrast: 16.51% in urban areas and 33.63% in rural areas. Extreme poverty reached 6.99% nationally, with 3.69% in urban areas and 14.07% in rural areas.

These estimates are computed from public ENEMDU microdata and compared against public annual INEC tabulations for analytical reproducibility. This is not an official institutional validation.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Why this monitor exists
Public poverty data in Ecuador are valuable, but the reading is often spread across bulletins, microdata, technical notes, methodological reports, and isolated tables.
The problem is not the absence of data. The problem is that the story connecting the data is often fragmented.
The Ecuador Poverty Monitor is designed as a reproducible communication layer. It does not replace official statistics or create a parallel statistical authority. It helps readers understand what is measured, how it is measured, what can be compared, and where each estimate has limits.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
The core idea
A useful poverty monitor should not only ask how many people are poor.


Condition
Income, deprivation, and the relative position of households.


Place
Territory, cities, rurality, and valid domains of estimation.


Measurement
Precision, representativity, and comparability across periods.

title: Poverty is not a single number.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Product status

This site is under construction.
This version establishes the editorial, visual, and methodological foundation. Future phases will add data-building scripts, charts, final infographics, and page-specific analysis.



Official microdata
Built around public ENEMDU source files.


Methodological contracts
Periods and domains are explicit before analysis.


Statistical quality
Representativity and precision guide what can be shown.


Reproducible output
Quarto consumes derived products, not raw microdata.

title: Entender la pobreza más allá de una sola cifra.
text:
Capa ejecutiva en español



El sitio principal está escrito en inglés para funcionar como producto de portafolio internacional. Esta página resume la propuesta para lectores ecuatorianos y audiencias locales.
El objetivo es explicar pobreza, territorio, empleo y calidad estadística con una narrativa reproducible, sobria y útil.

title: Entender la pobreza más allá de una sola cifra.
text:
Para qué sirve
El Ecuador Poverty Monitor no reemplaza estadísticas oficiales ni crea una autoridad paralela. Su función es convertir información pública compleja en una lectura ordenada, visual y reproducible.
Ayuda a responder qué se está midiendo, qué comparaciones son válidas y qué límites tiene cada estimación.

title: Entender la pobreza más allá de una sola cifra.
text:
Cápsula anual 2025
En 2025, la pobreza por ingresos alcanzó 21,95% a nivel nacional. La brecha territorial es marcada: 16,51% en el área urbana y 33,63% en el área rural. La pobreza extrema fue de 6,99% a nivel nacional, 3,69% en el área urbana y 14,07% en el área rural.

Estos resultados se calculan a partir de microdatos públicos de ENEMDU y se comparan con tabulados públicos anuales del INEC con fines de reproducibilidad analítica. No constituyen validación institucional oficial.

title: Entender la pobreza más allá de una sola cifra.
text:
Tres capas


Fuente
ENEMDU y documentación metodológica oficial.


Motor
Procesamiento reproducible mediante enemduR.


Producto
Narrativa editorial, gráficos e infografías futuras.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
Why it matters




The problem is not the lack of data. The problem is the missing reading layer.

Poverty data in Ecuador already exist across official bulletins, microdata, methodological notes, poverty lines, labor reports, monthly releases, quarterly files, and annual databases. This monitor turns that scattered public information into a coherent, reproducible, and decision-oriented reading.




The Ecuador Poverty Monitor connects income, deprivation, territory, labor conditions, and statistical quality into one static Quarto product.
It does not replace official statistics. It helps explain how to read them.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
The Ecuador Poverty Monitor connects income, deprivation, territory, labor conditions, and statistical quality into one static Quarto product.
It does not replace official statistics. It helps explain how to read them.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
What is fragmented?
The issue is not a shortage of information. The issue is that each release answers only part of the question.
Tables can inform, but poverty analysis needs context: what was measured, what period supports the estimate, which territorial domains are valid, and how precise the result is.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
What the monitor connects
Poverty can be read through income, basic needs, simultaneous deprivation, territory, and work. Each lens illuminates a different part of the problem.
When those pieces are read separately, the picture remains incomplete. When they are connected, the country becomes easier to understand.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
Source, engine, product
ENEMDU is Ecuador’s National Survey of Employment, Unemployment, and Underemployment. It is one of the country’s key statistical operations for analyzing work, labor conditions, income, poverty, and household characteristics.


1. Official source
The base information comes from ENEMDU and its methodological documentation.


2. Analytical engine
enemduR organizes reading, validation, estimation, indicators, and analytical outputs.


3. Narrative product
This site turns results into a visual and explanatory story.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
Three speeds, different questions
This product does not treat all periods as if they supported the same type of reading. The monitor is organized into monthly pulse, quarterly city view, and annual structural analysis.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
Golden rule
Never show a territorial disaggregation that does not match the representativity domain of the period being used. Methodological clarity is part of the product, not a secondary note.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
A compact product contract
This monitor adds an integrated reading of poverty, a reproducible architecture based on enemduR, a stronger visual communication layer, a clear distinction between pulse and structure, and a technical discussion of representativity.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
Not a parallel authority
The monitor does not replace INEC or produce official statistics. Its goal is to transform complex public information into an ordered, reproducible, visual, and useful reading of poverty in Ecuador.
